---
title: "sammy_r21 — clustering"
subtitle: "Per-sample Leiden clustering and QC on the UMAP"
format:
  pptx:
    reference-doc: ouhsc_ppt_template.pptx
    # `#` becomes a Section Header slide, `##` a Title-and-Content slide. Without
    # this pandoc infers the level from the headings present, so adding the first
    # `#` would silently demote every `##` to a bullet.
    slide-level: 2
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  # Keep rendering when a cell raises: sample sections are hardcoded, so a section
  # whose h5ad was not staged errors on its own slide instead of taking the whole
  # deck down. The cost is that a genuinely broken cell yields an error slide and an
  # exit code of 0 — read the slides, not just the exit status.
  error: true
---

```{python}
# | tags: [setup]
from pathlib import Path

import anndata as ad
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import display, Markdown
from matplotlib.colors import LogNorm

# The reference template is 16:9 at 10 x 5.625in; leave room for a slide title.
FIGSIZE = (9.0, 4.3)

# Every h5ad is staged into this notebook's working directory by the Nextflow
# process, so globbing is the whole input contract — no params to pass or drift.
# These are cluster's h5ads, not create_adata's: they carry X_umap and the leiden
# columns, and their cells are already past the mito cut.
H5ADS = sorted(Path(".").glob("*.h5ad"))
if not H5ADS:
    raise FileNotFoundError("No .h5ad files staged next to the notebook.")

# Cap the panels per row so a wide sweep wraps instead of shrinking to nothing.
MAX_COLS = 3


def load_sample(path):
    """Read one h5ad's obs and UMAP coords without pulling X into memory.

    backed="r" leaves the count matrix on disk. obs and obsm both come into memory
    regardless, which is what this report needs and is a few MB per sample against a
    cohort X of ~5 GB.
    """
    adata = ad.read_h5ad(path, backed="r")
    df = adata.obs.copy()
    umap = adata.obsm["X_umap"]
    df["umap_1"] = umap[:, 0]
    df["umap_2"] = umap[:, 1]
    adata.file.close()
    return df


def resolution_columns(df):
    """The sweep's leiden columns, ordered by resolution rather than by string.

    Discovered from obs rather than hardcoded: --resolutions is a pipeline param, so
    the set of columns is whatever the run that produced these h5ads chose. Sorting
    on the float matters — a plain sort puts leiden_1.0 before leiden_0.2.
    """
    cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("leiden_")]
    return sorted(cols, key=lambda c: float(c.split("_", 1)[1]))


def panel_grid(n, figsize=FIGSIZE):
    """A balanced grid sized to exactly n panels, with any leftover axes removed.

    Rows first, then columns back-solved from the row count, so the panels spread
    evenly instead of filling rows to MAX_COLS and stranding a remainder: 4 metrics
    land 2x2 rather than 3 across with one alone under a two-thirds gap. 6 still
    lands 2x3.

    delaxes, not set_visible(False): a hidden axes still reserves its cell under
    tight_layout, which squeezes the real panels into a fraction of the slide and
    strands the labels. Removing it reclaims the space.
    """
    nrows = int(np.ceil(n / MAX_COLS))
    ncols = int(np.ceil(n / nrows))
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows, ncols, figsize=figsize, squeeze=False)
    flat = axes.ravel()
    for ax in flat[n:]:
        fig.delaxes(ax)
    return fig, flat[:n]


def style_umap(ax, title):
    """UMAP axes carry no interpretable units — drop the frame, keep the title."""
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=8, loc="left")
    ax.set_xticks([])
    ax.set_yticks([])
    ax.spines[["top", "right", "bottom", "left"]].set_visible(False)


# (column, panel title, log colour scale). Counts span orders of magnitude, so a
# linear ramp puts almost every cell in the bottom colour; the rest are bounded.
QC_METRICS = [
    ("n_genes_by_counts", "Genes detected", False),
    ("total_counts", "UMI counts", True),
    ("pct_counts_mt", "Mitochondrial %", False),
    ("doublet_score", "Doublet score", False),
]


def qc_umap(sample):
    """One slide's figure: this sample's UMAP, once per QC metric.

    A continuous viridis ramp here, deliberately unlike the qc_report scatter's
    banded mito colours. Those bands exist to pick a cutoff, so they put the
    candidate numbers in the legend. That question is settled and already applied —
    every cell here is under 50% — so the question is now "does a region of the
    manifold run high", which is a magnitude read, and a ramp answers it. Rebanding
    at 30/40/50 would leave three colours and mostly grey.

    Debris and doublets are what to look for: cluster.py deliberately applies no
    gene/count floor, so the low-gene cells are still here and expected to sit apart.
    """
    d = samples[sample]
    fig, axes = panel_grid(len(QC_METRICS))

    for ax, (metric, title, log_c) in zip(axes, QC_METRICS):
        values = d[metric]
        # Guard the log norm: a zero or negative would raise, and total_counts can be
        # small on debris. vmin at the smallest positive value keeps the ramp honest.
        norm = LogNorm(vmin=max(values[values > 0].min(), 1e-9), vmax=values.max()) if log_c else None
        scatter = ax.scatter(
            d["umap_1"], d["umap_2"], c=values,
            cmap="viridis", norm=norm,
            # Opaque: a colorbar inherits its mappable's alpha and would draw the
            # ramp blended toward white.
            s=2, alpha=1.0, edgecolors="none", rasterized=True,
        )
        style_umap(ax, title)
        cbar = fig.colorbar(scatter, ax=ax, fraction=0.045, pad=0.02)
        cbar.ax.tick_params(labelsize=6)

    fig.suptitle(f"{sample} · {len(d):,} cells", fontsize=9, x=0.01, ha="left")
    fig.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    plt.close(fig)


def cluster_umap(sample):
    """One slide's figure: this sample's UMAP, once per Leiden resolution.

    The point of the slide is choosing a resolution, so the panels are the sweep and
    the titles carry the cluster count. Labels are drawn on the clusters rather than
    in a legend: at 25 clusters a legend would eat the panel, and tab20 has to cycle
    anyway — the on-data label is what actually identifies a cluster, so colour only
    has to separate neighbours.
    """
    d = samples[sample]
    res_cols = resolution_columns(d)
    if not res_cols:
        raise ValueError(f"{sample}: no leiden_* columns in obs — was this h5ad clustered?")

    fig, axes = panel_grid(len(res_cols))
    cmap = plt.get_cmap("tab20")

    for ax, col in zip(axes, res_cols):
        labels = d[col].astype(str)
        # Numeric order so the colour a cluster gets is stable across resolutions,
        # and so the legend-free labels read in order.
        levels = sorted(labels.unique(), key=int)
        for i, level in enumerate(levels):
            m = labels == level
            ax.scatter(
                d.loc[m, "umap_1"], d.loc[m, "umap_2"],
                color=cmap(i % cmap.N),
                s=2, alpha=1.0, edgecolors="none", rasterized=True,
            )
            # Median, not mean: a cluster with a stray arm would drag the mean off
            # the cells it is labelling.
            ax.annotate(
                level,
                (d.loc[m, "umap_1"].median(), d.loc[m, "umap_2"].median()),
                fontsize=5, fontweight="bold", ha="center", va="center",
                color="black",
                bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.1", facecolor="white",
                          alpha=0.7, edgecolor="none"),
            )
        style_umap(ax, f"{col.replace('leiden_', 'resolution ')} · {len(levels)} clusters")

    fig.suptitle(f"{sample} · {len(d):,} cells", fontsize=9, x=0.01, ha="left")
    fig.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    plt.close(fig)


def md_table(df):
    """Render a DataFrame as a markdown table.

    Built by hand rather than via df.style (HTML-only, renders as nothing in pptx)
    or df.to_markdown (needs tabulate, which the container does not carry).
    """
    header = "| " + " | ".join(str(c) for c in df.columns) + " |"
    rule = "| " + " | ".join("---" for _ in df.columns) + " |"
    rows = ["| " + " | ".join(str(v) for v in row) + " |" for row in df.itertuples(index=False)]
    display(Markdown("\n".join([header, rule] + rows)))


samples = {}
for path in H5ADS:
    df = load_sample(path)
    samples[df["sample"].iloc[0]] = df

# Order samples by condition then id so the table reads as two blocks — the same
# order the hardcoded sections below are in.
meta = pd.DataFrame(
    [(s, d["condition"].iloc[0], int(d["id"].iloc[0])) for s, d in samples.items()],
    columns=["sample", "condition", "id_num"],
).sort_values(["condition", "id_num"])
sample_order = meta["sample"].tolist()
```

## Clusters per resolution

```{python}
# One row per sample, one column per resolution: the cluster counts the per-sample
# slides show one at a time, in the one place they can be compared. Not hardcoded —
# it iterates sample_order, so it picks up samples with no edit here.
res_cols = resolution_columns(samples[sample_order[0]])
rows = []
for s in sample_order:
    d = samples[s]
    row = {"sample": s, "condition": d["condition"].iloc[0], "cells": f"{len(d):,}"}
    for col in res_cols:
        row[col.replace("leiden_", "res ")] = d[col].nunique()
    rows.append(row)
md_table(pd.DataFrame(rows))
```

<!--
Everything below is one hardcoded `# <sample>` block per sample, in samplesheet order
(condition, then ascending id — the same order sample_order produces). Regenerate them
from assets/samplesheet.csv rather than typing ids by hand.

Hardcoding is deliberate, not laziness. Generating the headings in a loop needs
`output: asis`, and under it an inline figure lands between the markdown blocks and
leaves the *next* heading not starting its own line — so pandoc emits a literal
"## sample" as text and drops the image. One cell per sample means plt.show() just
works. The summary table above is the opposite case: it iterates sample_order and so
picks up new samples with no edit here.
-->

# normal_id_1

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_1")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_1")
```

# normal_id_3

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_3")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_3")
```

# normal_id_9

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_9")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_9")
```

# normal_id_17

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_17")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_17")
```

# normal_id_20

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_20")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_20")
```

# normal_id_27

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_27")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_27")
```

# normal_id_33

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_33")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_33")
```

# normal_id_36

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_36")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_36")
```

# normal_id_55

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_55")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_55")
```

# normal_id_61

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("normal_id_61")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("normal_id_61")
```

# obese_id_23

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("obese_id_23")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("obese_id_23")
```

# obese_id_31

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("obese_id_31")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("obese_id_31")
```

# obese_id_57

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("obese_id_57")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("obese_id_57")
```

# obese_id_67

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("obese_id_67")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("obese_id_67")
```

# obese_id_75

## QC metrics on the UMAP

```{python}
qc_umap("obese_id_75")
```

## Clusters by resolution

```{python}
cluster_umap("obese_id_75")
```
